insights.insight: Ad tech charges $5–$150 CPA for modeled attribution. This protocol settles verified purchase confirmation at roughly $0.10 per receipt — a 50–200x cost delta that isn't a pricing negotiation, it's a different category of signal entirely.

With Chrome completing cookie deprecation and fingerprinting under regulatory pressure, the industry has no cross-ecosystem, identity-free purchase signal at scale — this dataset of 3,827 cryptographically verified receipts is the architecture that gap requires.

Kroger, Walmart, Costco, Target, Dollar General, and Home Depot now dominate the receipt corpus — meaning the harder data problem (physical, in-store, OCR-dependent) is being solved, not just digital email receipts.

Active purchase signals are appearing in Kentucky, Maine, Wisconsin, and rural Texas — not just coastal metro early-adopter clusters. The dataset is beginning to look like a national consumer panel, not a tech demo.

Amazon, Walmart, and your bank each see one slice of a consumer's purchase behavior. This protocol aggregates across all of them without identity attachment — the first cross-ecosystem view that no single retailer or data broker can replicate.

insights.topic: Receipt as conversion event, not proxy
insights.insight: Every signal in ad tech infers purchase intent; this protocol records purchase completion. The receipt is the conversion event itself, cryptographically signed — eliminating the attribution modeling layer that ad tech has been selling as a solution.
